history.hypothesis: Remove the `<personality>` section entirely since every instruction it contains is already stated in `<identity>` or `<guardrails>`, reducing noise without changing behavior.
history.critique: The baseline scores 1.0, so there's no failure to fix. However, the protocol contains redundancy that adds token cost without value. The `<personality>` section ("Disciplined craftsman. Follows blueprints. Raises ambiguity. Clean, minimal code. Ship it. Log everything.") restates themes already covered by `<identity>` ("disciplined craftsman who follows blueprints... log everything, stop cleanly") and `<guardrails>` ("On ambiguity: stop and ask"). This redundancy wastes agent attention on re-reading the same directives in slightly different words.

history.hypothesis: Add a concrete example of a good preflight approach outline in the `<preflight>` step so the agent produces more actionable pre-implementation summaries on varied tasks.
history.critique: The current protocol scores perfectly, so there's no failure to fix. However, the `<preflight>` step asks for a 3-5 bullet approach outline but gives no concrete example of what a good one looks like. On more complex tasks (beyond this benchmark), agents may produce vague or unhelpful preflight summaries that don't catch issues early enough, because they lack a model of what "good" looks like.

history.hypothesis: Move the branch check (step 5) to be the final startup step (after all reads are complete) so that startup flows logically from project context → task discovery → QA context → execution prep, eliminating the current interleaving of read and action steps.
history.critique: The current score is already 1.000 (perfect), and both previous experiments were reverted because they matched but didn't exceed it. The current startup sequence interleaves task discovery (step 4), execution prep (step 5, branch check), and task reading (steps 6-7, 10) rather than grouping them logically. Moving the branch check — which is an execution concern — to the end of startup, right before execution begins, creates a cleaner separation between "understanding the work" and "preparing to do the work."

Add a concrete example of a good preflight approach outline in the `<preflight>` step so the agent produces more actionable pre-implementation summaries on varied tasks.
